data.title: Viridien to Issue $950 Million of Bonds Due 2030
data.description:
Viridien on Monday launched an offering of senior secured notes due 2030 totaling $950 million.

The French geoscience company will use the proceeds of the US dollar and euro-denominated bonds to help redeem in full its senior secured notes due 2027, which amount to $447.4 million and 578.6 million euros.

Viridien also signed commitments for a $125 million revolving credit facility secured with the same collateral as the notes.

data.title: Market Chatter: 7-Eleven Owner, Couche-Tard Financial Advisers Looking for Potential Buyers for Some Stores
data.description:
The owner of 7-Eleven said financial advisers to the company and Alimentation Couche-Tard are searching for potential buyers for some stores to mitigate U.S. antitrust risk related to a potential takeover of the Japanese retail giant by the Canadian owner of Circle K, The Wall Street Journal reported overnight Sunday.

Seven & i Holdings said in a letter to shareholders Monday that Couche-Tard recently agreed to jointly explore a potential sale of some stores prior to signing a takeover agreement.

The 7-Eleven owner said that now is the right time to advance business initiatives in parallel with its discussion with Couche-Tard and that the company remains focused on identifying all other options to improve shareholder value.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally, and/or from other media sources. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Crypto shares fall, tracking weakness in bitcoin
data.description:
** Shares of cryptocurrency and blockchain-related companies down in premarket trading, as bitcoin falls

** Bitcoin , the world's biggest cryptocurrency, falls 4.6%, while Ether slides 2.2%

** Crypto exchange Coinbase Global falls 5.7%, U.S.-listed shares of blockchain farm operator Bitfarms down 3.2%

** Crypto miners Riot Platforms , Hut 8 Mining and Marathon Digital down between 4% and 5%

** Bitcoin miner CleanSpark slides 4.1%, while software firm and major crypto bull MicroStrategy falls 5.1%

data.title: Morgan Stanley Expects In-line FY24 Results for Italy's ERG
data.description:
ERG's (ERG.MI) net income for 2024 is set to broadly align with consensus at 198 million euros amid the adverse impact of "weak" wind speeds in the last quarter, Morgan Stanley said.

Before the release of the Italian renewable energy company's results on Tuesday, the analyst also projected 2024 dividend per share at 1.15 euros, against the guidance range of 1 euro per share to 1.3 euros per share.

For 2025, the analyst expects a 21% annual rise in EBITDA to 658 million euros, supported by higher Italy wind captured prices, expected capacity increases of 250 megawatts, full-year US contribution, and "stronger" power price environment.

The research firm has an underweight rating on the stock.

data.title: China Auto Sales Hit a New Record for February
data.description:
By Jiahui Huang

China's auto sales rose in February, setting a new record for the month thanks to the government's continued vehicle trade-in program and robust market sentiment after the Lunar New Year.

Retail sales of passenger cars rose 26% from a year earlier to 1.39 million vehicles last month, the China Passenger Car Association said Monday. Sales declined 23% from January, the agency said.

Despite a slow start to 2025, sales returned to strong growth in February, boosted by overall stronger consumer sentiment in China and extended subsidies for car owners to replace their old vehicles, the CPCA said. Additionally, the price war among Chinese automakers has been relatively soft this year, it said.

The electric-vehicle market remained a standout, with top EV makers experiencing strong sales and exports. February retail sales of new-energy cars, which include battery EVs and plug-in hybrids, rose 80% to 686,000 units.

The exports of passenger cars rose 11% to 349,000 units last month, the CPCA said. Exports of NEVs rose 28% to 118,000 units.

Tesla faced some pressure in its Chinese market in February. It sold 30,688 China-made vehicles during the month, down 49% from a year earlier and 51% from January, and exported 3,911 units, the agency said.

Cui Dongshu, secretary-general of the CPCA, said Tesla's export and sales declines could be due to political factors and the EV maker starting deliveries of the new Model Y in March. The CPCA remains confident about Tesla's future sales, Cui said.

The CPCA expects auto sales to remain robust in March, as carmakers often launch new models after the Lunar New Year. With local governments also offering some consumption-boosting policies, consumer sentiment is set to get a further lift in March, it said.

Write to Jiahui Huang at jiahui.huang@wsj.com

data.title: Philippines Manufacturing Output Rises 4% in January on Strong Food Production
data.description:
Philippine manufacturing output grew 4% year-on-year in January, accelerating from a 0.4% rise in December 2024, boosted by strong gains in food production, government data showed on Saturday.

The Value of Production Index (VaPI) rebounded from a 1.4% contraction in January 2024, with food manufacturing jumping 9.3%, up from a 1.4% increase the month before.

Food production accounted for 31.5% of the overall manufacturing trend. Growth in machinery and equipment (excluding electrical) and chemicals also supported the increase.

However, momentum weakened in February. The S&P Global Philippines Manufacturing Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) slipped to 51.0 in February from 52.3 in January, marking the slowest expansion in 11 months.

Softer demand and output weighed on the index.

Adding pressure, Glencore suspended operations at its Pasar smelter in the Philippines, highlighting challenges in the copper sector as Chinese smelters capture a larger share of global refining capacity.

China now controls about half of the world's copper smelting capacity.

data.title: Report Supports Case for Rio Tinto Unification in Australia, Palliser Says
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker and Rhiannon Hoyle

Activist investor Palliser Capital said a report that it commissioned into Rio Tinto's corporate structure supports its view that the global miner should abandon its primary London listing and unify its corporate structure into a single Australian-domiciled company.

Palliser, a U.K. hedge fund, engaged Grant Thornton Australia to examine whether the advantages of unification outweigh the disadvantages for both U.K. and Australian investors.

The report's conclusions were published ahead of a vote by Rio Tinto shareholders on Palliser's resolution calling for an independent review into its listing status. Rio Tinto's U.K.-listed shareholders will vote on April 3, while its Australian-listed shareholders will vote on May 1.

In a letter sent to Rio Tinto's chairman on Monday, Palliser said the report bolsters its case that the mining company was wrong to reject its calls to unify and urge its shareholders to vote against the resolution.

Rio Tinto didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

Palliser, which owns a roughly $300 million stake in Rio Tinto, argued that the dual-listed structure destroys shareholder value, prevents stock-based mergers and acquisitions, and keeps the miner trading at a steep discount to peers.

Rio Tinto, however, previously said a review it conducted last year found that its dual-listed company structure continues to be effective and provide benefits to the company and shareholders. Rio Tinto said it periodically reviews the DLC, but its board undertook an especially deep review of the DLC structure in 2024, which came to those conclusions.

Grant Thornton's report concluded unification would make Rio Tinto better able to pursue stock-based deals and conduct share buybacks. Rio Tinto executives have rejected the idea that the existing structure is an obstacle to dealmaking.

Unification would deliver a near-term benefit to U.K.-listed shareholders and, over the long run, be neutral or value accretive for Australian shareholders, the report said.

Rio Tinto said its current structure supports the group's shareholder returns and is demonstrated by the fact that, in London, it is one of the top five dividend payers in the FTSE 100. Rio Tinto's board previously said they believe Palliser's claims are unfounded and misleading.

Rio Tinto's own review last year included substantial input from Goldman Sachs and J.P. Morgan as external financial advisers, and Linklaters and Allens as legal advisers, while a detailed tax analysis was undertaken by EY, the miner said.

Grant Thornton also concluded that the increased proportion of Australian shareholders post-unification would allow for the greater use of franking credits. These refer to tax credits passed on by Australian companies to their shareholders with dividend payments.

The tax costs associated with unification have been contested by Palliser and Rio Tinto. The miner believes they will be in the mid-single-digit billions of U.S. dollars. However, Grant Thornton found Palliser's view that unification may trigger one-off transaction costs of around $450 million and additional tax costs of around $145 million a year to be reasonable.

Grant Thornton's analysis of shareholder registers suggests there would likely be broad-based support for unification amongst Rio Tinto shareholders based on their significant overlap with shareholders of companies that recently voted to unify.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com and Rhiannon Hoyle at rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com

data.title: The 10-Point: The Wall Street Journal's Guide to the Day's Top News
data.description:
By Emma Tucker

Russia is accelerating its attempt to eject Ukrainian forces from their toehold in its Kursk region, adding pressure ahead of a meeting between Ukraine and U.S. officials. In Washington, a close-knit group of Trump advisers and lobbyists from Florida are shaping the president's second-term agenda. And clients of women's rights attorney Gloria Allred say her public image as a feminist crusader clashes with her persona behind the scenes.

Today's Headlines

Russia's advance to retake Kursk threatens Ukraine's hold on territory that Ukrainian officials had hoped would give them leverage in any peace talks.

Mark Carney won the leadership of Canada's Liberal Party, putting him in line to replace Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.

Federal immigration officers have detained a Columbia University student who helped lead last year's pro-Palestinian demonstrations.

Gaines County, Texas — the epicenter of a measles outbreak — exemplifies pockets of America where antivaccine sentiment has surged.

Utah is set to become the first state to ban fluoride in its public water systems.

Live From The Markets

Follow our live financial coverage all day.

Southern yellow pine futures are coming to Wall Street as prices for it split from those of Canadian lumber.

This week marks the 25th anniversary of the Nasdaq's dot-com-era peak. All wasn't lost in the ensuing downturn, writes Rolfe Winkler.

Read It Here First

In Trump's Washington, it pays to be from Florida.

Many of President Trump's closest advisers, including his secretary of state, national security adviser, attorney general and chief of staff, have roots in Florida politics. Numerous other Floridians have taken jobs across the administration. And a group of high-powered Florida Republican operatives are converting their Sunshine State clout into Washington influence. Alex Leary, Josh Dawsey and Maggie Severns examine the circle of Floridians powering some of Trump's initiatives.

Gloria Allred used high-pressure tactics — on her own clients.

The lawyer's lifelong crusade against sexual misconduct, representing women accusing Jeffrey Epstein, Bill Cosby, Harvey Weinstein and others of misdeeds, branded her as a champion of women. But behind the scenes, clients say, Allred and her firm ignored their wishes and pushed them to agree to confidential settlements. Some clients now question whether retaining the firm was in their best interest, both emotionally and financially, and whether decisions were made primarily to benefit Allred and her firm, Khadeeja Safdar reports.

Expert Take

Q: The U.S. paused military aid to Ukraine. Can NATO fill the gap?

The Trump administration has halted crucial weapons deliveries and intelligence sharing with Ukraine. The prospect of Washington cutting all its support to Kyiv has prompted European NATO members to step up and also take a hard look at their arsenals. Daniel Michaels explains in this video why U.S. capabilities are critical to Europe.

A: The U.S. has spent decades building up a military that is both massive and technologically far ahead of anybody else. Because of that — and because European defense plans were built on the assumption that the U.S. would be part of it — there are many areas that Europe just hasn't invested in.

Capabilities, especially for Ukraine, are vital, and there are certain ones that the U.S. brings that Europe cannot replace, including air-defense systems and long-range rocket artillery. Many components and systems essentially went out of production after the Cold War, so Europe is trying to rebuild an industry that hasn't been in place in 30-something years. When Russia launched its large-scale invasion of Ukraine, Europe rebooted its defense industry from almost a standing start.

While European NATO members are taking steps to strengthen their arsenals, they aren't improving as fast as the Ukrainians or as many Europeans want. And now with the added pressure from Washington, Europe needs to move even faster but isn't in shape to do that.

See The Story

China is waging a 'gray zone' campaign to cement power.

From the choppy waters of the South China Sea and Taiwan Strait to the frozen ridges of the Himalayas, China is pursuing a relentless campaign of expansion, pushing boundaries in what security strategists call the "gray zone." Years of ship-tracking data, flight paths and satellite images show a clear intensification of tactics meant to intimidate rivals and deepen China's control.

Happening Today

China's new tariffs on U.S. food and agricultural products are set to take effect.

Earnings: Oracle, Vail Resorts

Number Of The Day

23%

The share of U.S. tech jobs posted so far this year that are seeking employees with artificial-intelligence skills, job-listings data show, as firms in nearly every corner of the economy adjust their recruiting pipelines to embrace the technology.

And Finally. . .

The latest car technology is starting to drive people nuts.

Beyond touch screens and assisted-driving systems, vehicles now come equipped with infrared night vision, seasonal ambient lighting and interior "fam cams" showing rear passengers. Many drivers say the tech is too much.

data.title: Lianhua Supermarket Expects to Considerably Narrow Loss in 2024
data.description:
Lianhua Supermarket expects to record a net loss of about 340 million yuan to 380 million yuan for 2024, compared with 791 million yuan in the same period a year earlier, according to a Friday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

The company attributed the narrower loss to no longer recognizing losses from an associate company in 2024 after fully writing off its investment in 2023 and closing long-term loss-making outlets to cut expenses and improve resource allocation.

The China-based supermarket operations business plan to publish their annual results in March.

The company's shares were down over 7% at the close of trade.

data.title: US Stocks Brief: S&P Futures Down Near 1% and Nasdaq As President Trump Says Economy Faces "Transition" and Treasury Sec. Scott Bessent Says "There's Going To Be a Detox Period"
Gold steady as firm dollar offsets safe-haven demand
data.description:
Key points:
  • Gold could correct further, analyst says
  • US CPI data due on Wednesday, PPI data on Thursday
  • China's deflationary pressures deepen in February

Gold steadied on Monday as a stronger U.S. dollar countered safe-haven demand amid trade war concerns, while investors looked to inflation data this week for clues on the Federal Reserve's next interest rate decision.

Spot gold was at $2,913.09 an ounce at 0946 GMT, while U.S. gold futures firmed 0.2% to $2,920.10.

The dollar index held above last week's four-month low, making gold more expensive for holders of other currencies.

Quantitative Commodity Research analyst Peter Fertig said a rise in the dollar is weighing on bullion and he expects a further correction to below $2,900.

Meanwhile, market focus remains on trade tensions. In his latest warning to Canada, U.S. President Donald Trump said on Friday that reciprocal tariffs on dairy and lumber could be imminent.

Gold's success in holding above $2,900 reflects concerns about the wider economic picture and an ongoing heightened geopolitical risk environment, Frank Watson, market analyst at Kinesis Money, said in a note.

Traders are looking to U.S. Consumer Price Index (CPI) data on Wednesday and the Producer Price Index (PPI) on Thursday for U.S. interest rate cues.

The Fed has held interest rates so far this year after cutting three times in 2024. Market pricing reflects expectations of a further cut in June. (FEDWATCH)

Bullion is seen as a hedge against inflation and geopolitical uncertainty but higher rates can dent the non-yielding asset's appeal.

Data showed top metals consumer China's consumer price index missed expectations in February and fell at the sharpest pace in 13 months, while producer price deflation persisted.

Spot silver edged up 0.2% to $32.59 an ounce, platinum rose 1% to $973 and palladium was up 0.5% at $952.68.

About TBC Bank Group PLC ("TBC PLC")

TBC Bank Group's mission is to make people's lives easier by providing digitally-led financial services to retail and corporate customers. TBC Bank Group PLC ("TBC PLC") is a public limited company registered in England and Wales and is the parent company of TBC Bank Georgia and TBC Uzbekistan.

TBC Bank Georgia, together with its subsidiaries, is the leading financial services group in Georgia, with a total market share of 38.5% of customer loans and 38.1% of customer deposits as of 31 December 2024, according to data published by the National Bank of Georgia.

TBC Uzbekistan is the leading digital banking ecosystem in Central Asia with 18 mln unique registered users that includes TBC Bank Uzbekistan, the country's largest mobile-only bank, Payme, a leading digital payments app for individuals and small businesses, and Payme Nasiya, an instalment credit business.

TBC PLC is listed on the London Stock Exchange under the symbol TBCG and is a constituent of the FTSE 250 Index. It is also a member of the FTSE4Good Index Series and the MSCI United Kingdom Small Cap Index.
